Theory doesn't distinguish market wisdom from market madness - relabels both as 'efficient'

positions.title: Stocks as Claims on Imagined Futures Not Intrinsic Utility
positions.thesis: Unlike commodities with intrinsic utility (wheat, copper), stocks are claims on imagined futures with no intrinsic use, only expectations. Their price depends on mood, narrative, and belief - making them more like luxury goods than commodities.
source: WORK-040 ('Priced In': The Circular Faith of the EMH)
key_arguments: Commodities anchored in physical necessity - wrong price causes scarcity/surplus
Stocks have no intrinsic use, only expectations
Price depends on whimsical, irrational, fleeting factors
EMH calls all of this 'information' no matter how arbitrary

positions.title: EMH as Theology of Price Not Theory of Value
positions.thesis: EMH is theology of price - inviting trust in markets because everything is priced in. It confuses knowledge with movement, rationality with reaction, truth with trend, turning 'pricing in' from useful heuristic into circular article of faith.
source: WORK-040 ('Priced In': The Circular Faith of the EMH)
key_arguments: EMH makes you trust the market not because it's rational but because it defines rationality
Descriptive in loose sociological way but lacks normative/predictive power
Cannot distinguish insight from inertia, value from fashion
Need to recover signal-noise, insight-inertia, value-fashion distinctions

positions.title: Star Wars as Jungian Dreamscape
positions.thesis: Star Wars (1977) is not space opera but Jungian dreamscape - every frame steeped in archetype, guided by mythic structure, powered by psychological machinery Jung mapped. About soul, not space.
key_arguments: Lucas openly credited Joseph Campbell (who was really just Jung with storytelling manual)
Luke's arc is pure individuation process
Film uses archetypal figures: Shadow (Vader), Wise Old Man (Kenobi), Anima (Leia)
The Force represents collective unconscious

positions.title: Hero's Journey as Individuation Map
positions.thesis: Jung's individuation (integration of all aspects of self: conscious, unconscious, shadow, anima/animus) is what hero's journey really maps - not story template but internal transformation.
key_arguments: Luke starts in ignorance/naïveté (undeveloped consciousness)
Receives call to adventure (awakening of deeper self)
Meets guides, faces trials, awakens to inner power
Confronts and integrates the Shadow

positions.title: Darth Vader as The Shadow
positions.thesis: In Jungian terms, Vader represents the Shadow - the denied, repressed, projected part of self that is powerful, dangerous, and essential. He is the unclaimed part of Luke - what Luke could become if he gives in to power without balance.
key_arguments: Shadow is not evil per se but unintegrated dark
Vader is masked, cloaked, unknowable - symbolically what's repressed
Ultimate arc is Luke confronting and refusing to destroy Shadow, reaching out to redeem it
This is Jung's core teaching on integration

positions.title: The Force as Collective Unconscious
positions.thesis: The Force is Jungian metaphor: impersonal, morally ambiguous, accessible through intuition, binding everything transcending language. It's deep substrate of shared symbolic life, not God or science.
key_arguments: Jedi tap into it like mystic/shaman taps into collective unconscious
When Kenobi says 'Trust your feelings,' issuing Jungian directive
Force represents non-rational knowing through deep psychic substrate
Accessed through intuition not intellect

positions.title: Star Trek as Freudian Psychic Diagram
positions.thesis: Star Trek: TOS wasn't just sci-fi but psychological diagram - Kirk/Spock/McCoy map exactly onto Freud's ego/superego/id and his three libidinal character types (narcissist/obsessional/libidinal).
key_arguments: Kirk = Ego/Narcissist (rational mediator, doer, craver of glory)
Spock = Superego/Obsessional (reason, logic, restraint, repression)
McCoy = Id/Libidinal (emotion, passion, care, body)
Each episode stages conflict between facets of single psyche

positions.title: Kirk as Ego - Mediator Between Impulse and Restraint
positions.thesis: Kirk embodies Freudian ego and narcissist type: brash, confident, improvisational, making decisions and taking responsibility. He navigates between Spock's logic and McCoy's sentiment, integrating and synthesizing to act.
key_arguments: Listens to Spock but doesn't submit to logic
Feels with McCoy but doesn't drown in sentiment
Carries burden of command - must integrate both poles
Oriented toward action, visibility, impact (narcissist in Freudian sense)

positions.title: Spock as Superego - Harshest Internal Judge
positions.thesis: Spock is superego embodied: reason, logic, restraint through repression of human half. He's obsessional character - intellectual, self-disciplined, defending against emotion. Embodies Freud's observation that superego is harshest judge of self.
key_arguments: Suppresses emotion with surgical precision
Aspires to purity that alienates him
Not at war with others but with himself
Everything filtered through principle

positions.title: McCoy as Id - Voice of Passion and Body
positions.thesis: McCoy is not primitive brute but voice of passion, outrage, care, body - the libidinal type and structural id. He expresses energy, anger, yearning that others (especially Spock) suppress.
key_arguments: Emotional, drinks, snaps, loves, mourns, feels
Argues with Spock because insists on human stakes
Doesn't give orders - gives a damn
Conscience in raw and bodily register

positions.title: Star Trek's Psychological Symmetry as Source of Durability
positions.thesis: Star Trek's brilliance isn't stories but psychological symmetry: id pushes for passion, superego demands restraint, ego must choose/balance/synthesize/act. Each episode is microcosm of self in conflict.
key_arguments: Real frontier isn't outer space but psyche
Show mapped psyche in Freudian terms
Feels intimate despite galactic scale because it's about internal conflict
Endures because about making peace within

positions.title: Cultural Hollowness Laments as Status Anxiety
positions.thesis: When writers lament 'the hollowness of our times' (Eliot, Sartre, Camus, anti-capitalist intellectuals), they're not describing historical condition but their own status anxiety - they feel displaced by mass culture, new money, technology, change.
source: WORK-040 (Hollowness as Code for Status Anxiety)
key_arguments: They never specify what exactly was lost (church? monarchy? guild system? illiteracy?)
Point isn't accuracy but to signal seriousness and status
Take refuge in elegy because they knew where they stood in old world
Broadcasting allegiance to cultural aristocracy that no longer exists

positions.title: Snobbery as Performance of Half-Safe Position
positions.thesis: Snobbery is something junior people do to impress senior people - 'please like me' disguised as 'I am above you.' It's the culture of the half-safe. When you're actually fighting for your life, you have no bandwidth for class-posturing.
source: WORK-040 (Hollowness as Code for Status Anxiety)
key_arguments: Snobbery is for people trying to secure place in shifting cultural order
Henry Ford, Bezos, Musk are not snobs - they're not trying to impress anyone
Snobbery dies when self-reliance begins
The Henry Ford Principle: more responsibility you carry, less room for snobbery

positions.title: Status Anxiety Disguised as Historical Diagnosis
positions.thesis: Hollowness these writers see in world is really just echo of their own fading status. Their 'diagnosis' is job interview disguised as cultural criticism.
source: WORK-040 (Hollowness as Code for Status Anxiety)
key_arguments: Don't feel securely respected in plural, chaotic, decentralized age
Feel displaced and take refuge in mourning what's 'lost'
Not because it's better but because they knew their place in that world
Way to broadcast cultural aristocracy credentials

positions.title: Satire Fails Without Systems Understanding
positions.thesis: Literary satire falls apart if you don't understand the system you're mocking. Writers like Lewis portray middle-class work as absurd without understanding capital flows, risk underwriting, legal frameworks, infrastructure coordination.
key_arguments: Lewis sneers at real estate, boosterism, commerce, insurance, mortgage brokering
But none could exist without complex economic machinery
Writers lack knowledge of how economies work
Fine until they start scoffing at people who do have this knowledge

positions.title: The Babbitt Problem: Mocking Components as Clowns
positions.thesis: Babbitt may be windbag but he occupies place in real machinery of civilization - helps allocate space, facilitates growth, is node in economic network. Lewis doesn't see this because he never bothers. To him Babbitt is type not participant, clown not component.
key_arguments: Can't mock what you don't understand
Lewis sees Babbitt as shallow theater
But Babbitt helps coordinate real economic activity
Satire rings hollow without understanding of system function

positions.title: The Frank Burns Problem: Mocking the Machinery That Makes Everything Possible
positions.thesis: Hawkeye mocks Frank Burns, but Burns follows procedure and keeps system running. Hawkeye mocks the machinery, but it's the machinery that makes surgery possible. Satire without respect equals superficiality.
key_arguments: Burns may be cowardly, conservative, self-important - but he works
Follows procedure that maintains functional system
If you don't understand how housing markets, banks, zoning boards work, you're not writing critique - just doing stand-up
Laughter from people who've never had to fix the plumbing

positions.title: Freud's Misreading of American Vitality as Spiritual Hollowness
positions.thesis: Freud treated American vitality (efficiency, productivity, optimism) as mask for decay rather than different mode of health. He had no serious understanding of how economies work and couldn't see that 'hollowness' was actually mass coordination, energy management, risk absorption at unprecedented scale.
key_arguments: Freud said America's project to build civilization without brooding introspection was 'doomed to fail'
He saw denial of inner conflict, ignorance of repression, worship of efficiency
But mistook pragmatic action-oriented culture for shallowness
Never laid bricks, sold houses, or ran payroll - picture rooted in high culture not social mechanics

positions.title: American Aspiration vs European Repression as Different Health Modes
positions.thesis: Freud believed psychic health came from facing darkness. America was full of people building rather than brooding. For Freud this looked like evasion. But maybe it was civilization powered not by repression but by aspiration.
key_arguments: Roads don't pave themselves, markets don't coordinate themselves
Someone has to do the doing
America didn't fail Freud's standards - Freud brought wrong standards
What looked like hollowness was civilization in motion

positions.title: Snobbery as Epistemological Vice
positions.thesis: Snobbery is not just social vice but epistemological one - a kind of built-in blindness. Lewis's portrayal of Babbitt suffers from defensiveness dressed up as superiority, presumption of guilt that ruins ability to see clearly.
key_arguments: Snobbery entails presumption of guilt: Babbitt ridiculous because he's suburbanite, Rotarian, Republican
His inner life doesn't matter - already weighed and found wanting
Contempt is enemy of perception - can't understand what you've decided to look down on
Snobbery is performative contempt that replaces curiosity with judgment

positions.title: The Anthropologist Analogy for Bad Satire
positions.thesis: Imagine anthropologist who despises tribe he's meant to observe - thinks rituals stupid, beliefs primitive. Maybe feels threatened by their strength, cohesion, joy, so adopts supercilious posture. How much will he learn? Nothing. His fieldwork is already over.
key_arguments: Whatever good is there - he won't see it
That's what happens with Lewis - Babbitt isn't examined but diagnosed
Moments where Babbitt breaks through as real are accidental - Lewis's snobbery tries to suppress them
Satire weaker than subject because attack too shallow to be fatal

positions.title: Appeals Process as Ritual Audit Not Truth-Seeking
positions.thesis: Appeals courts don't ask 'Was justice done?' but 'Were the rules followed?' They evaluate procedural/legal errors, not actual innocence. Legal system views trial as ritual and appellate process as ritual audit - concern is whether form was maintained, not whether defendant is innocent or guilty.
source: WORK-040 (Why Appeals Courts Don't Care If You're Innocent)
key_arguments: You don't appeal the verdict, you appeal the trial
New evidence (confession, videotape, DNA) often not enough without procedural mechanism
Extraordinary procedures (habeas, clemency) are rare, discretionary, high bar
Innocent can rot in prison if paperwork was in order

positions.title: Scalia and Rawlsian Proceduralism: Fairness in Structure is Enough
positions.thesis: Legal system embodies belief that fairness in structure is enough - if the system was followed, justice has been done. This is not only wrong in theory but devastating in practice, allowing innocent to be imprisoned while courts say 'paperwork was in order.'
source: WORK-040 (Why Appeals Courts Don't Care If You're Innocent)
key_arguments: System doesn't exist to find truth - it exists to follow procedure
Reflects Scalia/Rawlsian proceduralism worldview
Not justice but bureaucratic theater
People trapped inside have no exit unless system voluntarily opens door

positions.title: Bach's Music as Engineering Not Just Expression
positions.thesis: Bach's music isn't just expressive but engineered - in same sense as suspension bridge or cathedral. His fugues and chorales are systems: harmonic architectures, counterpunctual machines, vaults of sound built with structural intelligence and terrifying precision. This IS German engineering in musical form.
key_arguments: Not like German engineering but IS German engineering musically
Fugues are not free-floating emotional outbursts but systems
Built with structural intelligence matching architectural/mathematical thought
Sound of mind building something that will stand centuries

positions.title: Music as Intelligible Emotion - Soul Through System
positions.thesis: Music at its best is intelligible emotion - emotion made knowable through form. What happens when human feeling is passed through filter of logic, structure, constraint and transformed into something enduring. Emotion is energy, structure is geometry, music is fusion.
key_arguments: Music is not merely raw expression of feeling or aesthetic entertainment
Requires discipline of feeling through form
Bach doesn't just move us - he instructs us
Shows reconciliation of soul and system

positions.title: Structural Music Emerges Only in Civilizations with Engineering/Logic
positions.thesis: We don't get music of enduring formal complexity in cultures lacking corollary in engineering, architecture, mathematics, or theology-as-logic. Wherever civilization has deep commitment to structure, it gives rise to music reflecting that commitment.
key_arguments: Examples: India's tāla systems, Arabic maqamat, Gregorian chant, Western classical tradition
Bach is crowning moment of structural musical tradition
When structure disappears from civilization (no theology, architecture, philosophy, engineering), music doesn't evolve
Becomes mere ornament, collapses into noise, or commodified background hum

positions.title: Music as Civilizational Diagnostic
positions.thesis: If you want to know where a civilization stands, don't look at its politics - look at its music. Music reveals whether civilization has structure, logic, theology, engineering, or has collapsed into formless expression.
key_arguments: Music is window into civilization's structural commitments
Formless music indicates civilization without structure
Complex formal music indicates deep commitment to logic/engineering/theology
Better diagnostic than political analysis

positions.title: Reverse Brain Engineering as New Epistemic Modality
positions.thesis: Building AI systems that approximate human cognition is not merely technical feat but fundamentally new way of knowing - a reflexive, co-constructive epistemic loop between creator and creation that transcends deduction, induction, and abduction.
key_arguments: Traditional epistemology relies on deduction, induction, abduction
AI systems that mirror cognitive life enable real-time bidirectional reflexive interaction
Epistemic intimacy - systems talk back, probe assumptions, ask questions
This is co-constructive, not just interactive

positions.title: AI as Epistemic Partner Not Amplifier
positions.thesis: Unlike telescope or particle accelerator (epistemic amplifiers that strengthen senses), AI systems theorize back. They embody theories in dynamic unfolding way, forcing us to confront limitations of our own cognition, intuitions, philosophical assumptions.
key_arguments: Traditional instruments amplified observation but didn't theorize
AI systems help test theories AND embody them dynamically
In interacting with them we confront our own cognitive limits
This is new epistemic modality beyond classical categories

positions.title: Recursive Co-Modeling as New Form of Knowledge Generation
positions.thesis: Reverse brain engineering is recursive co-modeling - dialogical process where line between subject and object, knower and known, begins to blur. When we build artificial minds that help us understand our own, we create mirrors that talk back.
key_arguments: Cannot be fully captured by deduction, induction, or abduction
Contains elements of all three but transcends them
We don't just create tools - we create mirrors
Mirrors then talk back, creating recursive loop

positions.title: AI Development as Epistemological Turning Point
positions.thesis: Just as writing changed not just what we knew but how we knew, construction of artificial minds marks turning point in epistemology. We're no longer simply thinking about mind - we're thinking with it in new synthetic form.
key_arguments: Promises to reshape understanding of intelligence
Will change conception of how knowledge is generated
Writing analogy: changed method not just content
This makes all the difference

positions.title: OCD as Adaptive Machinery in Overdrive
positions.thesis: OCD is not just pathology but adaptive machinery pushed into overdrive. Built from traits that in right amounts helped ancestors survive: error detection, threat anticipation, long-range consequence modeling, rule-following, mental rehearsal, moral self-monitoring.
key_arguments: These aren't flaws but survival tools - cognitive seatbelts
When working properly: stop poisoning tribe, prevent violating taboos, maintain purity in rituals, protect kin, model social/moral consequences
Person with OCD might once have been tribe's safety system
Double-checked perimeter, insisted on cleanliness, mentally simulated threats

positions.title: OCD Prototype in Prehistoric Context
positions.thesis: 50,000 years ago in tight-knit band, someone who double-checked perimeter, insisted on ritual cleanliness, mentally simulated threats repeatedly, felt guilty after minor transgressions might have been liability in some ways but godsend in others - especially if compulsions aligned with group needs.
key_arguments: Hygiene, purity, ritual weren't superstitions but frameworks for disease avoidance and cohesion
Every action had stakes - mistakes with food, fire, boundaries could be fatal
OCD person was early warning system
Noticed what others missed, cared too much but saved lives in danger

positions.title: OCD as Misapplied Boundary-Maintenance System
positions.thesis: In modern abstract, crowded, digital world, machinery that maintained physical/moral boundaries has no natural object. So turns inward, fixates on trivialities, creates imagined scenarios to defuse through ritual. System isn't broken - it's misapplied, doing its job too well in wrong context.
key_arguments: No longer live in village, kill game, perform blood rituals
Precision module is hyper-functioning
Moral radar with gain turned up
Threat simulator that never powers down

positions.title: OCD as Sacred Function Misfiring
positions.thesis: You don't get OCD from nowhere - you get it from same architecture that in another life might have made you priest, scout, healer, watchman. Pain is real but mind isn't broken - it's just tuned for world that no longer exists.
key_arguments: Instincts (cleanliness, guilt, doubt, checking, correcting) are old tools in noisy modern world
May hurt now but once protected something sacred
In right frame, they still can
OCD sufferer carries vestige of protective sacred role

positions.title: Economics Not Self-Contained Predictive System
positions.thesis: Economics is not self-contained system that can predict itself. It's downstream of culture, politics, religion, personality, invention. Charismatic leader can redirect nation's economy in week. War, famine, singularity make mockery of best economic models. These events aren't noise - they're constitutive.
key_arguments: Economics isn't ground floor - it's shadow on wall of deeper forces
New religious movement alters consumption patterns of millions
Technology breakthrough restructures entire sectors
'Exceptions' aren't exceptions - they are the rule

positions.title: Economics vs Biology: Probabilistic vs Non-Self-Predictive
positions.thesis: Biology is probabilistic but not arbitrary - state variables constrain future through non-random mechanisms. Economics not even robustly probabilistic. System never was self-predictive to begin with. Not that it's sound but occasionally disrupted - it's that it was never self-predictive.
key_arguments: Biological probabilities underwritten by immune function, genetic stability, cellular repair
Economics has no such robust mechanistic base
Economic 'laws' constantly violated by non-economic forces
Forecasting fails not because inputs noisy but because model refuses to acknowledge what it's observing

positions.title: Inca Example: Economics Records But Doesn't Explain Non-Economic Systems
positions.thesis: To predict Inca behavior in 1457, look at 1456 rituals, laws, cosmic beliefs, cosmological metaphors - not marginal utility curves. Economics may record food stores and track trade but won't tell why workers redeployed to sacred site, surplus burned instead of stored, labor restructured to appease eclipse.
key_arguments: To model as 'distortion' of economic rationality is fundamental misunderstanding
Not deviation from economics but non-economic system with own internal logic
Economic forecasting fails because underlying model tries to predict shadows cast by events it refuses to acknowledge
Economics isn't wrong because imperfect - it's wrong because thinks it's primary when it isn't

positions.title: Serial Killerdom as Specific Cognitive-Behavioral Protocol
positions.thesis: Serial murder is not random violence but premeditated, patterned, highly stylized behavior with limited recurring types (organized/disorganized, visionary/hedonistic, power/mission-oriented). This taxonomic stability suggests it's specific cognitive-behavioral protocol, not generic trauma response.
key_arguments: Serial killers have victim types, fantasize for years, ritualize, repeat
Criminologists consistently identify same patterns
Implies biological/evolutionary basis
Not trauma-caused but trauma-triggered - activates pre-existing architecture

positions.title: Serial Killerdom Not Modern Invention But Modern Category
positions.thesis: Serial murder has long existed (Gilles de Rais, Elizabeth Báthory, slaughtering priests of empires). What's new isn't behavior but recognition as distinct category - product of modern bureaucratic state with tools to track, name, mythologize recurring patterns.
key_arguments: Concept of 'serial killer' is modern bureaucratic creation
Behavior is ancient
Modern state can track patterns across jurisdictions
Creates category from previously unconnected instances

positions.title: Trauma as Trigger Not Cause of Serial Killing
positions.thesis: Many serial killers had disturbing upbringings but 'trauma = serial killer' doesn't track. Most abuse victims don't become serial murderers. Some notorious killers didn't experience abuse exceeding ordinary dysfunction. Trauma is trigger sparking very particular pre-existing architecture.
key_arguments: If trauma caused it, world would be overrun
Like synesthesia or savant syndrome - neural structure is latent, conditions activate it
Something pre-installed, waiting
Biographies don't suffice as explanation

positions.title: Serial Killerdom as Evolutionary Spandrel or Vestigial Adaptation
positions.thesis: If behavior is specific, structured, eerily reproducible, must ask where it came from. Evolution repurposes, mutates, experiments with hyper-specialists. Serial killerdom perhaps: spandrel (byproduct of adaptive features), vestigial adaptation, or extreme failure mode of modules serving critical social functions.
key_arguments: Component traits (ruthlessness, emotional detachment, deception, pattern-driven aggression) may have been selectively advantageous
In warfare, territorial conflict, covert enforcement
Evolution didn't select FOR serial killing
But in ancestral contexts, component traits were useful

positions.title: Serial Killers as Overclocked Predator Mode
positions.thesis: What sets serial killers apart isn't violence but method: they stalk, hunt, blend in, mimic normal social behavior with chilling precision. Not moral deformity but overactivation of predator mode made grotesque by misfiring reward circuits and unregulated internal narratives.
key_arguments: Many have vivid fantasy lives preceding crimes by years - rehearse and refine
Capacity for internal rehearsal and abstraction made humans apex species
Serial killers are not broken animals but overclocked machines
Carrier of protocol that should not be active but is

positions.title: Economic Theory as Intellectual Colonization
positions.thesis: When Western-trained economist attempts to manage non-Western economy, doesn't observe or listen but immediately translates: 'What's your interest rate? Marginal productivity? Utility function?' None make sense in native conceptual world, but economist is there to re-code not learn.
key_arguments: Real face of intellectual colonization: equations and utility curves imposed on cultures not recognizing those categories
When living economic system (with rituals, temporal rhythms, taboos, meanings) forcibly mapped onto Western model, something essential is lost
Not theoretical worry - happens over and over
Bad translation masquerading as understanding

positions.title: Flattening Sacred into Economic Data
positions.thesis: Incan administrator describes labor system organized around sacred seasons, familial debt, reciprocal land stewardship. Economist replies: 'Ah yes, non-monetary kinship-based tax structure with redistributive efficiency function.' But that's not what it is - that's just bad translation. The ritual is no longer ritual, sacred no longer sacred.
key_arguments: This is flattening - converting meaning into data
Real damage begins when modeler believes he understands more than culture he's modeling
Now he not only translates but intervenes
'Must increase productivity - opportunity cost of rituals too high - shift labor to raise GDP by 2%'
GDP in society without money

positions.title: When Model Becomes Reality: Metaphysical Violence
positions.thesis: When economists attempt to predict or restructure alien economies using only their own conceptual vocabulary, they aren't doing science - they're doing metaphysical violence. Converting world into format their models can digest, discarding everything else as noise.
key_arguments: Models can be useful - clarify and simplify
But when model becomes reality, no longer describing but distorting
To understand society, must first allow it to remain strange
Must see economy not as market-in-waiting but as living form of life

positions.title: Humility vs Equations in Understanding Alien Systems
positions.thesis: If you want to understand a society, must allow it to remain strange - see its economy as living form of life not market-in-waiting. Takes humility, not equations. Without humility, economic theory isn't tool but colonizer with calculator.
key_arguments: Need to see system on its own terms first
Then can apply frameworks if appropriate
But framework should serve understanding not replace it
Economics without humility is imperialism

positions.title: Material Conditional Does Not Capture "If-Then" Meaning
positions.text: The symbol "→" in classical logic (material conditional) does not mean "if...then" in natural language. It merely means "not both P and not-Q" and is strictly truth-conditional, lacking the modal/entailment force of natural language conditionals.

positions.title: Natural Language Conditionals Are Modal
positions.text: "If P, then Q" in natural language means "Q follows from P" or "not-Q is incompatible with P" - a modal notion involving possibility and impossibility, not reducible to truth-functional logic.

positions.title: The Paradoxes of Material Implication Are Not Genuine Paradoxes
positions.text: The so-called "paradoxes of validity" (e.g., that a false statement entails anything) are not real paradoxes but arise from misconstruing "P→Q" as meaning "if P, then Q" when it actually only means "not (P and not-Q)".

positions.title: Relevance Logic Misguided in Approach
positions.text: Attempts by relevance logicians (Belnap, Anderson) to create truth-conditional approximations of natural language "if...then" are misconceived, as they try to model a fundamentally modal notion using truth-conditional methods.

positions.title: Classical Logic Implicitly Requires Non-Truth-Conditional Conditionals
positions.text: Classical logic is implicitly committed to a non-truth-conditional conception of conditionality, as the Law of Non-contradiction depends on the modal notion that no statement can be compatible with its own negation.

positions.title: Utilitarianism Is Self-Defeating
positions.text: Utilitarianism undermines itself because it presupposes moral bonds and commitments (like those that make Erskin's family deserving of money) while simultaneously permitting violation of those same kinds of bonds when they conflict with utility maximization.

positions.title: Kantian Ethics Implicit in the Concept of Desert
positions.text: The concept of what people "deserve" inherently presupposes a Kantian ethical framework where people are ends-in-themselves and not mere means. Without this, the concept of desert loses meaning.

positions.title: Promise-Keeping Cannot Be Overridden by Utility Calculations
positions.text: If it is morally permissible to break one promise for the sake of greater utility, then the moral bonds that create all obligations (including those that make certain people "deserve" help) are undermined. The permission to violate commitments destroys the ground of all moral desert.

positions.title: Marriage and Parenthood as Moral Commitments
positions.text: Marriage and parenthood constitute special moral commitments that create obligations not owed to others. These commitments are of the same kind as promises and cannot be selectively enforced without contradiction.

positions.title: The Greatest Good Presupposes Anti-Utilitarian Ethics
positions.text: The utilitarian goal of "the greatest good for the greatest number" presupposes a world where people can trust each other, exercise autonomy, and not be defrauded - but this presupposes exactly the kind of non-consequentialist ethics that utilitarianism rejects.

positions.title: Moral Worth of Persons Is Precondition for Any "Greater Good"
positions.text: There cannot coherently be a "greater good" in a world where people lack inherent moral worth and can be treated as mere means. The concept of a greater good depends on people having the kind of value that prohibits treating them merely as means.

positions.title: Consequentialism Generally Is Self-Undermining
positions.text: All forms of consequentialism face the same problem as utilitarianism: they permit actions that undermine the very conditions (trust, autonomy, respect for persons) that make their goals valuable.

positions.title: Sensory Perception Is Reliable
positions.text: We can trust our senses as sources of knowledge. Skeptical arguments against sensory perception fail.

positions.title: Cartesian Skepticism About Senses Can Be Refuted
positions.text: Descartes' skeptical arguments about sensory perception and the evil demon hypothesis can be successfully refuted.

positions.title: We Have Certain Knowledge
positions.text: Despite skeptical challenges, there are things we can know with certainty.

positions.title: The Problem of Induction Has a Solution
positions.text: Hume's problem of induction can be resolved. (Note: Kuczynski's broader work argues for a rationalist a priori solution)

positions.title: Gettier Cases Show Justification Requires Non-Accidental Connection to Truth
positions.text: Gettier's examples demonstrate that justified true belief is insufficient for knowledge. Knowledge requires that the connection between justification and truth not be accidental.

positions.title: Testimony Requires Independent Verification for Knowledge
positions.text: For testimony-based belief to constitute knowledge, one must have independent evidence of the testifier's reliability. Testimony alone is insufficient.

positions.title: We Have Access to Absolute Truth
positions.text: There exists such a thing as absolute truth, and we can know it. Relativism about truth is false.

positions.title: Uncertainty Has Philosophical Value
positions.text: Recognition of uncertainty and the limits of knowledge has important philosophical value (despite our ability to know some things absolutely).

positions.title: Bostrom's Simulation Argument Deserves Serious Consideration
positions.text: Nick Bostrom's argument that we may be living in a computer simulation raises genuine epistemological questions worthy of philosophical analysis.

positions.title: Mind-Body Dualism Has Strong Arguments
positions.text: There are compelling arguments for substance dualism - the view that mind and body are fundamentally different kinds of substances.

positions.title: Materialism Faces the Hard Problem of Consciousness
positions.text: Materialist accounts of mind face severe difficulties explaining subjective conscious experience (the "hard problem").

positions.title: Nagel's Argument Against Materialism Is Sound
positions.text: Thomas Nagel's famous argument (from "What Is It Like to Be a Bat?") successfully refutes materialist theories of mind.

positions.title: Subjective Experience Cannot Be Reduced to Physical Facts
positions.text: The qualitative, subjective nature of conscious experience (qualia) cannot be fully captured or explained by physical/functional descriptions.

positions.title: The Explanatory Gap Is Real
positions.text: There is a genuine explanatory gap between physical/functional descriptions of brain states and phenomenal consciousness that materialist theories cannot bridge.

positions.title: Dennett's Eliminativism About Consciousness Is False
positions.text: Daniel Dennett's attempt to eliminate or explain away phenomenal consciousness fails. Consciousness is real and cannot be explained in purely functional/behavioral terms.

positions.title: Wittgenstein on Language and Thought Contains Important Insights
positions.text: Wittgenstein's views on the relationship between language and thought raise important philosophical questions about meaning and understanding.

positions.title: Referential-Attributive Distinction Is Not Russellian
positions.text: The distinction between referential and attributive uses of descriptions requires a non-Russellian treatment. Russell's theory of descriptions cannot adequately capture this distinction.

positions.title: The Hypothetico-Deductive Method Has Serious Problems
positions.text: The hypothetico-deductive method of scientific confirmation faces significant philosophical difficulties that undermine its status as the primary method of scientific reasoning.

positions.title: Values Play an Essential Role in Science
positions.text: Value judgments are not eliminable from scientific practice. Science necessarily involves value assumptions that go beyond purely empirical matters.

positions.title: Modern Science Has Distinctive Epistemic Features
positions.text: Modern scientific method differs fundamentally from pre-modern approaches to knowledge in ways that have important philosophical implications.

positions.title: Early Modern Scientific Thought Represents Philosophical Breakthrough
positions.text: The scientific revolution of the early modern period (Galileo, Newton, etc.) represents a fundamental shift in philosophical thinking about nature and knowledge.

positions.title: Moore's Proof of an External World Fails
positions.text: G.E. Moore's famous proof of an external world (by holding up his hands) is unsuccessful and does not refute skepticism about the external world.

positions.title: There Is an External World
positions.text: Despite problems with Moore's proof, we can establish that an external world exists.

positions.title: Descartes' Conception of Self Is Defensible
positions.text: Descartes' view of what he is (a thinking thing) can be defended, though aspects require modification.

positions.title: Standard Arguments for God's Existence Fail
positions.text: The traditional arguments for God's existence (cosmological, teleological, ontological) are unsuccessful.

positions.title: There Are Genuine Paradoxes Relating to God
positions.text: The concept of God as traditionally understood generates genuine paradoxes (e.g., omnipotence paradox, problem of evil) that create serious philosophical difficulties.

positions.title: God Is Not Necessary for Morality
positions.text: Morality does not depend on God's existence. Ethical truths and obligations exist independently of whether God exists.

positions.title: God's Foreknowledge Conflicts with Moral Responsibility
positions.text: If God has complete foreknowledge of all future events, human beings cannot be genuinely morally responsible for their actions. Divine foreknowledge and human free will are incompatible.

positions.title: Agnosticism Is More Defensible Than Theism
positions.text: Given the arguments for and against God's existence, agnosticism is a more philosophically defensible position than belief in God.

positions.title: Religious Institutions Face a Fundamental Paradox
positions.text: Religious institutions face an inherent paradox in attempting to systematize and institutionalize what is supposed to be a matter of personal faith and individual spiritual experience.

positions.title: Clifford Right About Epistemic Duty, William James Wrong
positions.text: W.K. Clifford's view that it is wrong to believe anything on insufficient evidence is correct, while William James' defense of the "will to believe" in religious matters is misguided.

positions.title: Dawkins' Critique of Religion Has Merit But Also Problems
positions.text: Richard Dawkins' arguments against religion contain valuable criticisms but also exhibit philosophical weaknesses.

positions.title: Wollstonecraft and Marx Correct About Religion's Social Function
positions.text: Mary Wollstonecraft and Karl Marx were right that religion serves problematic social functions, though their views differ in details.

positions.title: Galileo Was Right to Challenge Religious Authority on Scientific Matters
positions.text: Galileo was justified in asserting the autonomy of scientific investigation against religious authority.

positions.title: Searle's Chinese Room Refutes Strong AI
positions.text: John Searle's Chinese Room argument successfully demonstrates that mere computation/symbol manipulation is insufficient for genuine understanding and consciousness.

positions.title: Turing Test Is Not a Good Test of Intelligence
positions.text: The Turing Test (Imitation Game) is not an adequate test for genuine intelligence or consciousness. Behavioral indistinguishability from humans does not establish presence of mind.

positions.title: Free Will Is Compatible with Determinism
positions.text: Free will and determinism are compatible (compatibilism). We can act freely even if our actions are determined by prior causes.

positions.title: Hume, Frankfurt, and Holbach on Freedom Raise Important Issues
positions.text: The views of Hume, Harry Frankfurt, and Baron d'Holbach on personal freedom and responsibility raise important questions about the nature of human agency.

positions.title: Neuroscience Does Not Eliminate Free Will
positions.text: Recent neuroscientific findings (e.g., Libet experiments) do not actually disprove the existence of free will.

positions.title: Operant Conditioning Does Not Disprove Free Will
positions.text: The existence of operant conditioning and behavioral modification does not establish that we lack free will.

positions.title: Consciousness and Free Will Are Compatible
positions.text: There is no conflict between the phenomenology of consciousness and the existence of free will.

positions.title: We Have Access to Our Own Mental States Through Introspection
positions.text: We have privileged first-person access to our own mental states through introspection. This access is direct and generally reliable.

positions.title: Apes Lack Key Cognitive Capacities Humans Possess
positions.text: Despite significant cognitive abilities in great apes, humans possess distinctive cognitive capacities (likely related to language and abstract thought) that apes lack.

positions.title: Mill's Harm Principle Defensible
positions.text: John Stuart Mill's harm principle (that liberty should only be restricted to prevent harm to others) is fundamentally sound.

positions.title: Liberty Has Intrinsic Value
positions.text: Personal freedom and liberty have inherent value, not merely instrumental value for producing other goods.

positions.title: Hobbes, Marx, Rousseau, Nietzsche Each Identify Central Problems
positions.text: Hobbes' analysis of the state of nature, Marx's critique of capitalism, Rousseau's social contract theory, and Nietzsche's critique of conventional morality each identify important truths about politics and society.

positions.title: Free Markets Have Both Virtues and Vices
positions.text: Free market systems produce important benefits (efficiency, innovation, wealth creation) but also generate serious problems (inequality, exploitation, market failures).

positions.title: Legal Freedom and Substantive Freedom Can Conflict
positions.text: There is a tension between legal/formal freedom (absence of coercion) and substantive freedom (actual ability to pursue one's goals). The free market can maximize the former while limiting the latter.

positions.title: Mill and von Hayek Make Important Contributions to Economic Philosophy
positions.text: Both Mill's and Friedrich von Hayek's analyses of free market economics contain valuable insights, despite their different emphases.

positions.title: Founding Fathers Had Complex Views on Democracy
positions.text: The American founding fathers both supported and opposed democratic elements. The Constitution was neither simply democratic nor simply anti-democratic but mixed.

positions.title: Plato's Republic Prefigures Totalitarianism
positions.text: Plato's ideal state in the Republic shares disturbing features with modern totalitarian regimes (echoing Karl Popper's famous critique).

positions.title: Freedom of Speech Has Limits
positions.text: While freedom of speech is valuable and important, it is not absolute. There are legitimate limits to free expression.

positions.title: Freedom of Speech Deserves Strong Defense
positions.text: Despite legitimate limits, freedom of speech requires robust defense against attempts to restrict it for bad reasons.

positions.title: Prejudice Functions as Psychological Resistance
positions.text: Prejudice and bigotry serve psychological functions (such as maintaining self-esteem, creating in-group solidarity, projecting unacceptable impulses onto out-groups).

positions.title: Freudian Analysis Illuminates Prejudice
positions.text: Psychoanalytic concepts (sublimation, projection, displacement) help explain the psychological roots of prejudice and discrimination.

positions.title: College Admissions Should Consider Standardized Tests
positions.text: Standardized tests should continue to play a role in college admissions despite their limitations, as they provide valuable information about academic preparation.

positions.title: Wealth Redistribution Can Be Legitimate
positions.text: Under certain conditions, government redistribution of wealth is morally justified.

positions.title: Drug Legalization Has Economic Justification
positions.text: There are strong economic arguments for legalizing currently prohibited drugs.

positions.title: Environmental Regulation Preferable to Carbon Taxes
positions.text: Direct environmental regulations are superior to carbon tax schemes for addressing environmental problems.

positions.title: Minimum Wage Laws Are Economically Harmful
positions.text: Minimum wage laws, despite good intentions, produce negative economic consequences that harm those they intend to help.

positions.title: American Entry into WWI Had Economic Justification
positions.text: The United States' entry into World War I can be justified on economic grounds.

positions.title: US Was a World Leader 1946-1991
positions.text: The United States functioned as a genuine world leader during the Cold War period, despite problematic aspects of its foreign policy.

positions.title: Colonialism Differs from Imperialism
positions.text: Colonialism and imperialism, while related, are distinct phenomena that need to be analyzed separately.

positions.title: Soviet Union Collapse Had Multiple Causes
positions.text: The collapse of the Soviet Union resulted from a combination of economic, political, and social factors rather than any single cause.

positions.title: Late-Term Abortions Raise Serious Ethical Issues
positions.text: Late-term abortions present more difficult ethical questions than early-term abortions, requiring careful philosophical analysis.

positions.title: Suicide Raises Complex Philosophical Questions
positions.text: Schopenhauer's analysis of suicide and related philosophical discussions show that the ethics of suicide is more complex than commonly assumed.

positions.title: Corporate Executives Should Be Held Criminally Liable for Workplace Deaths
positions.text: When corporate negligence leads to deaths or injuries in the workplace, executives should face criminal rather than merely civil penalties.

positions.title: Euthanasia and Medical Ethics Require Careful Analysis
positions.text: End-of-life medical decisions, including euthanasia, raise complex ethical issues that demand rigorous philosophical examination.

positions.title: Business Ethics Faces "Endgame" Scenarios
positions.text: Business people regularly face ethical dilemmas where maintaining ethical principles seems to conflict with competitive success, requiring philosophical analysis of whether ethics and business success are compatible.

positions.title: Camus and Schopenhauer Share Views on Meaning of Life
positions.text: Albert Camus and Arthur Schopenhauer reach similar conclusions about the meaninglessness or absurdity of life, despite different philosophical frameworks.

positions.title: Camus and Nagel on Life's Meaning Converge
positions.text: Camus' absurdism and Thomas Nagel's analysis of life's meaning share important insights about the human condition.

positions.title: Camus' Hero Is Rebel Without Traditional Cause
positions.text: Camus' vision of authentic existence involves rebellion against absurdity without appeal to traditional religious or metaphysical meaning.

positions.title: Schopenhauer on Human Suffering Remains Relevant
positions.text: Schopenhauer's analysis of human suffering and the will contains insights that remain philosophically important.

positions.title: Schopenhauer's Metaphysics Has Fractal Structure
positions.text: Schopenhauer's conception of reality involves a fractal-like structure where the same patterns repeat at different levels.

positions.title: Nietzsche and Schopenhauer on Meaning Differ Importantly
positions.text: While both Nietzsche and Schopenhauer deny traditional religious meaning, their alternative visions differ significantly.

positions.title: Sartre's Later Work Inferior to Earlier Work
positions.text: Sartre's later Marxist writings are philosophically weaker than his earlier existentialist work.

positions.title: Socrates' Execution Involved Multiple Factors
positions.text: Socrates was executed not simply for his philosophical views but due to a complex combination of political, social, and philosophical factors.

positions.title: Pre-Socratics Identify Problem of the One and the Many
positions.text: The pre-Socratic philosophers identified a fundamental metaphysical problem about unity and plurality that remains important.

positions.title: Plato's Cave Allegory Addresses Epistemology and Metaphysics
positions.text: Plato's allegory of the cave makes important points about both the nature of knowledge and the structure of reality.

positions.title: Aristotle's Virtue Ethics Has Enduring Value
positions.text: Aristotle's approach to ethics through virtue and character development provides an important alternative to consequentialism and deontology.

positions.title: Aristotle's Conception of Good Life Remains Relevant
positions.text: Aristotle's analysis of eudaimonia (flourishing/the good life) offers insights applicable to contemporary questions about wellbeing.

positions.title: Aristotle on Virtue More Defensible Than Kant
positions.text: Aristotle's virtue-based approach to ethics is philosophically superior to Kant's duty-based approach in important respects.

positions.title: Kant's Moral Philosophy Has Significant Strengths
positions.text: Kant's ethical theory, based on the categorical imperative and treating persons as ends, captures important moral truths.

positions.title: Kant's Formula of Humanity Captures Core of Morality
positions.text: Kant's principle of treating humanity always as an end and never merely as a means expresses a fundamental moral truth.

positions.title: Locke, Aristotle, and Kant on Virtue Can Be Synthesized
positions.text: The virtue theories of Locke, Aristotle, and Kant can be partially integrated despite their differences.

positions.title: Bentham and Mill on Pleasure Differ Importantly
positions.text: Jeremy Bentham's quantitative hedonism and John Stuart Mill's qualitative hedonism represent significantly different approaches to utilitarian ethics.

positions.title: Mill vs. Hobbes on Liberty Reveals Deep Disagreement
positions.text: Mill's defense of liberty and Hobbes' more restricted view of freedom reflect fundamentally different conceptions of human nature and politics.

positions.title: Rousseau, Goldman, and von Hayek Offer Competing Views of Ideal State
positions.text: Different political philosophers (Rousseau's general will, Emma Goldman's anarchism, Hayek's spontaneous order) present competing but each partially valid visions.

positions.title: Sociology of Knowledge Has Philosophical Importance
positions.text: Sociological factors in knowledge production raise important epistemological questions about objectivity and justification.

positions.title: Cartesian Certainty Possible But Limited
positions.text: We can achieve the kind of certainty Descartes sought, but only for a limited range of propositions.

positions.title: There Are Things We Know with Certainty
positions.text: Despite skeptical challenges, there are propositions we can know with absolute certainty (e.g., "I exist," "I am thinking").

positions.title: Global Warming Evidence Is Compelling
positions.text: The scientific evidence for anthropogenic climate change is strong enough to warrant serious concern and policy responses.

positions.title: Evolution Is Well-Established Scientific Fact
positions.text: The theory of evolution by natural selection is supported by overwhelming evidence and is as well-established as any scientific theory.

positions.title: Fiction Has Important Purpose
positions.text: Fictional narratives serve important functions beyond mere entertainment, including moral education, emotional development, and exploring possibilities.

positions.title: Modern Art Raises Philosophical Questions
positions.text: Works of modern art raise important philosophical questions about the nature of art, beauty, and aesthetic value.

positions.title: Language and Thought Are Intimately Connected
positions.text: Language and thought are deeply interconnected, though the exact nature of their relationship requires careful analysis.

positions.title: Intensionality and Modality Raise Pre-Semantic Issues
positions.text: Questions about intensional contexts and modal operators raise important pre-semantic considerations about meaning.

positions.title: Higher Education Has Distinctive Business Model
positions.text: Universities operate according to a distinctive business model that differs fundamentally from ordinary commercial enterprises.

positions.title: Graduation and Retention Rates May Not Matter Most
positions.text: Contrary to common assumptions, graduation and retention rates may not be the most important metrics for evaluating educational institutions.

positions.title: Universities Sell Degrees, Not Instruction
positions.text: The fundamental product universities offer is credentials (degrees) rather than education or instruction per se.

positions.title: Knowledge Management Principles Don't Apply to Traditional Universities
positions.text: Standard knowledge management principles used in business cannot be straightforwardly applied to traditional university structures.

positions.title: Digitization of Education Often Advisable
positions.text: Many forms of education can and should be digitized when possible for efficiency and accessibility.

positions.title: Alternative Education Models Should Be Explored
positions.text: Alternative models of higher education (like the hypothetical "DMO" described) deserve serious consideration as improvements over traditional university structures.

positions.title: Education Should Emphasize Instruction Over Selection
positions.text: Educational institutions should focus more on providing quality instruction to students than on functioning as gatekeepers selecting the most talented.

positions.title: Consequentialism Self-Defeating Generally
positions.text: The critique of utilitarianism extends to all forms of consequentialism: they undermine the conditions that make their goals valuable.

positions.title: Deontological Constraints Are Rationally Required
positions.text: Deontological constraints (such as prohibitions on treating persons as mere means) are not arbitrary restrictions on utility maximization but rational requirements of coherent moral thinking.

positions.title: Moral Bonds and Commitments Foundational
positions.text: Relationships involving trust, commitment, and special obligations (friendship, family, promises) are foundational to morality rather than being overrideable by impersonal utility calculations.

positions.title: Good Employee Treatment Not Always Profitable
positions.text: While treating employees well can sometimes increase profitability, there are cases where ethical treatment of workers reduces short-term profits, creating genuine ethical dilemmas for businesses.

positions.title: Corporate Social Responsibility More Than Profit Maximization
positions.text: Contra Milton Friedman, the social responsibility of business extends beyond mere profit maximization to stakeholders beyond shareholders.

positions.title: Money Can Function Like a Drug
positions.text: The pursuit of money can exhibit addiction-like psychological patterns that raise ethical questions about capitalism and human motivation.

positions.title: Parable of the Sadhu Reveals Ethical Failures
positions.text: The famous business ethics case "Parable of the Sadhu" reveals how people can fail to act ethically in group situations even when they would act ethically individually.

positions.title: Sublimation Remains Relevant Concept
positions.text: Freud's concept of sublimation (redirecting instinctual energy into socially acceptable outlets) remains psychologically and philosophically relevant.

positions.title: Failure to Sublimate Explains Some Pathologies
positions.text: The "Peter Pan syndrome" and related psychological patterns can be understood as failures of proper sublimation of impulses.

positions.title: Attachment Theory Illuminates Mental Illness
positions.text: Attachment theory provides important insights into the development and nature of various mental illnesses.

positions.title: Evolutionary Psychology Explains Why We Lie
positions.text: Lying and deception have evolutionary explanations related to adaptive strategies in social competition and cooperation.

positions.title: Internal vs. External Motivation Distinction Important
positions.text: The distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic motivation has important implications for psychology, education, and workplace management.

positions.title: Prejudice Has Deep Psychological Roots
positions.text: Prejudice and discrimination are not mere ignorance but have deep psychological roots in ego defense mechanisms and social identity formation.

positions.title: Feminist Theory Identifies Patriarchal Structures
positions.text: Feminist analysis successfully identifies systematic patterns of male dominance in social structures.

positions.title: Critical Thinking Faces Systematic Traps
positions.text: There are identifiable patterns of fallacious reasoning that systematically impede critical thinking and rational discourse.

positions.title: Free Market Creates Contradictions
positions.text: Free market capitalism generates internal contradictions that create economic instability and social problems.

positions.title: Marxist Economics Has Valid Insights
positions.text: Despite problems with Marxist political programs, Marxist economic analysis identifies real features of capitalism.

positions.title: Commodity Fetishism Is Real Phenomenon
positions.text: Marx's concept of commodity fetishism (treating social relations as relations between things) captures a real feature of market economies.

positions.title: Knowledge Capital Deserves Recognition
positions.text: Knowledge and information should be recognized as forms of capital deserving economic analysis.

positions.title: Technology-Caused Job Shortages May Require Welfare State
positions.text: If technology produces structural unemployment, universal welfare programs may become economically necessary.

positions.title: Philosophy Has Value Beyond Practical Application
positions.text: Philosophy is valuable not primarily for practical applications but for expanding understanding, reducing dogmatism, and exploring possibilities.

positions.title: Philosophical Uncertainty Has Value
positions.text: Recognition of the limits of our knowledge and the value of uncertainty is itself an important philosophical insight.

positions.title: Graduate-Level Philosophy Examines More Complex Issues
positions.text: Graduate philosophical work differs from undergraduate work in depth, complexity, and rigor of analysis rather than just in subject matter.

positions.title: Wittgenstein's Private Language Argument Is Flawed
positions.text: Wittgenstein's argument against private language fails because private rules can have enforcement mechanisms through natural consequences (e.g., eating poisonous berries if one misinterprets one's own code), not just social enforcement.

positions.title: Thought Not Inherently Linguistic
positions.text: Thought is not inherently linguistic. There exists visual thought, musical thought, and other non-linguistic forms of thinking. Wittgenstein was wrong to identify thought with language use.

positions.title: Thought Required for Language Understanding
positions.text: Thought is logically prior to language understanding. One must be able to think in order to understand or operate with semantic rules. Therefore, thought cannot be constituted by linguistic rule-following.

positions.title: Wittgenstein's Rule-Following Argument Fails
positions.text: The rule-following argument (that all genuine rules must be social/public) is false because private rules can have real consequences that constitute genuine enforcement without social mechanisms.

positions.title: Late-Term Abortions Specifically Are Unethical
positions.text: Late-term (third trimester) abortions are ethically wrong because neurobiological evidence shows late-term fetuses have psychological characteristics similar to newborn infants, making them persons with rights.

positions.title: Personhood Depends on Psychological Capacities
positions.text: Whether something is a person (and thus has moral rights) depends on whether it has relevant psychological capacities such as consciousness, pain sensitivity, and other mental features.

positions.title: Neurobiological Evidence Relevant to Abortion Ethics
positions.text: Medical/neurobiological evidence about fetal development is directly relevant to and decisive for ethical questions about abortion.

positions.title: "Future Like Ours" Argument Has Force
positions.text: Don Berkich's argument that fetuses have "a future like ours" and abortion robs them of this future provides a legitimate basis for opposition to abortion.

positions.title: Moore's Proof Begs the Question
positions.text: G.E. Moore's attempt to prove an external world by holding up his hands begs the question against the skeptic because the skeptic would not grant the existence of hands as mind-independent objects.

positions.title: Question-Begging Broader Than Premise-Conclusion Identity
positions.text: An argument begs the question not only when the conclusion is identical to a premise, but when the premises would not be granted by one's opponent or when accepting the premises requires already accepting the conclusion.

positions.title: Sophisticated Beliefs Are Distillations of Lower-Level Beliefs
positions.text: Beliefs like "there exists an external world" are sophistications or distillations of lower-level beliefs like "there exists a hand here." The fact that children have the latter without the former doesn't show independent plausibility.

positions.title: Non-Question-Begging Arguments Against Skepticism Possible
positions.text: Unlike Moore's proof, some arguments against skepticism don't beg the question, such as arguing from the existence of mental states plus causation principles to the existence of external causes of those mental states.

positions.title: Nagel's Bat Argument Succeeds Against Materialism
positions.text: Nagel's argument that complete knowledge of bat physiology would not give knowledge of what it's like to be a bat successfully refutes materialist accounts of consciousness.

positions.title: Physiological Knowledge Insufficient for Psychological Knowledge
positions.text: Complete knowledge of an organism's physical/physiological facts does not automatically give knowledge of that organism's phenomenal psychological states (what it's like to be that organism).

positions.title: Intensional Fallacy Response to Nagel Fails
positions.text: The objection that Nagel commits an intensional fallacy (like arguing water isn't H2O because one can know something is water without knowing it's H2O) fails because complete chemical knowledge gives complete knowledge of water, but complete physiological knowledge doesn't give complete knowledge of consciousness.

positions.title: Psychology Not Reducible to Physiology
positions.text: Psychology (what it's like to have certain experiences) cannot be reduced to or identified with physiology, even if they are in some sense correlated.

positions.title: Schopenhauer Correct That Life Involves Suffering
positions.text: Schopenhauer's analysis that life fundamentally involves suffering and that pleasure/happiness are unstable while pain is stable is essentially correct.

positions.title: Life's Meaning Requires Some Strife
positions.text: "Life without pain has no meaning" - some degree of adversity or challenge is necessary for life to have meaning (Schopenhauer's insight).

positions.title: Acceptance of Life's Negativity Enables Its Positive Aspects
positions.text: Both Camus and Schopenhauer agree that accepting what is negative or difficult in life is necessary for gaining what is positive from it.

positions.title: Camus' Absurdism Differs from Schopenhauer's Pessimism
positions.text: While both Camus and Schopenhauer have seemingly negative views of life, they differ fundamentally: Schopenhauer focuses on suffering, Camus on lack of pre-given meaning; and Camus sees lack of meaning as potentially liberating.

positions.title: Search for Pre-Given Meaning Prevents Finding Meaning
positions.text: Searching for what happiness or meaning consists of prevents one from being happy or living meaningfully (insight shared by Camus and Schopenhauer).

positions.title: Lack of Pre-Given Meaning Enables Freedom
positions.text: The fact that life doesn't have a pre-existing meaning is not purely negative; it allows human freedom to create meaning rather than being constrained by predetermined purposes (Camus).

positions.title: Professional Ethics Violations Include Breach of Confidentiality
positions.text: Violations of professional confidentiality (like discussing client information with others without consent) constitute serious ethical breaches regardless of motive.

positions.title: Professional Relationships Create Ethical Boundaries
positions.text: Romantic relationships between professionals and staff members can create ethical problems when they tempt violations of professional duties like confidentiality.

positions.title: Private Conduct Matters When It Affects Professional Duties
positions.text: While private conduct (like marital infidelity) may be morally problematic, it becomes professionally relevant primarily when it leads to breaches of professional responsibilities.

positions.title: Gettier Successfully Refutes JTB Analysis of Knowledge
positions.text: Edmund Gettier successfully showed that justified true belief is not sufficient for knowledge through his counterexamples.

positions.title: Knowledge Requires Non-Accidental Justification-Truth Connection
positions.text: The Gettier cases show that knowledge requires the connection between justification and truth to be non-accidental, not merely that both be present.

positions.title: No-False-Belief Condition Insufficient
positions.text: The amendment that knowledge = JTB with no false beliefs in the justification chain is insufficient, as shown by Goldman's fake barn case.

positions.title: Goldman's Fake Barn Shows Limits of No-False-Belief Amendment
positions.text: In Goldman's fake barn case, Jerry has JTB based on no false beliefs but arguably lacks knowledge due to environmental luck.

positions.title: Knowledge May Be Unanalyzable
positions.text: Williamson's claim that knowledge is a primitive, unanalyzable concept may be correct given the failure of various analyses.

positions.title: Assumption That All Concepts Are Analyzable Is Probably False
positions.text: The assumption that all concepts can be analyzed in terms of more basic concepts is almost certainly false; some concepts must be primitive.

positions.title: Knowledge Need Not Be Accompanied by Self-Knowledge
positions.text: One can know something without knowing that one knows it (contra certain ordinary language analyses). Meta-knowledge involves additional concepts beyond first-order knowledge.

positions.title: Radford's Knowledge-Without-Belief Claim Is Spurious
positions.text: Radford's claim that Jean knew but didn't believe that James I died in 1625 is wrong; Jean both knew and believed but lacked meta-knowledge.

positions.title: Subconscious Knowledge Counts as Belief
positions.text: Williamson's argument succeeds: When someone "guesses" based on subconscious memory, they both believe and know, even if not consciously aware.

positions.title: Bostrom's Simulation Argument Deserves Serious Consideration
positions.text: Nick Bostrom's argument that we may be living in an ancestor simulation run by posthuman civilizations raises genuine philosophical issues.

positions.title: Computational Substrate Independence Is Plausible
positions.text: The "comp" thesis that conscious experiences can be associated with any system implementing the right computational structures is plausible and marks an important difference from mere physical processes like hurricanes.

positions.title: Simulation Hypothesis Creates Epistemological Problems
positions.text: If fsim ≈ 1 (most observers with human-type experiences are in simulations), this creates significant epistemological challenges about knowing we're not simulated.

positions.title: Bostrom's "No Info" Assumption Goes Beyond What Stated
positions.text: Bostrom's "no info" assumption that we can't distinguish simulated from biological experiences assumes more than stated - it assumes something like Byrne's skeptical premise about indistinguishability.

positions.title: Experience Machine Is Bad for Multiple Reasons
positions.text: Nozick's experience machine thought experiment shows that we value not just experiences but also actually doing things and being certain kinds of people.

positions.title: Bostromian Simulation May Be Less Bad Than Experience Machine
positions.text: Living in a Bostromian ancestor simulation might not be as bad as Nozick's experience machine, depending on whether one values being "real" in a fundamental sense.

positions.title: Ethical Relativism Is False
positions.text: Ethical relativism (that what's ethical depends on culture) is false; there are universal moral truths that transcend cultural variation.

positions.title: Informed Consent Has Universal Value
positions.text: The principle of informed consent (patients must have relevant information to make medical decisions) has universal validity across cultures.

positions.title: Cultural Practices Must Be Evaluated by Universal Standards
positions.text: When cultural practices conflict with universal values like health and wellbeing, universal values should take precedence.

positions.title: Surface Cultural Values May Conflict with Deeper Universal Values
positions.text: Cultural injunctions (like Navajo prohibition on negative talk) may embody empirically false beliefs about how to actualize deeper, universal values shared across cultures.

positions.title: Medical Obligations Transcend Cultural Variations
positions.text: A physician's obligation to maximize patient health and minimize suffering is universal and not culturally relative, even if cultures impose conflicting obligations.

positions.title: Universal Values Exist Despite Cultural Differences
positions.text: While cultures differ in some values, certain values (like protecting life and health) are truly universal, though not universally complied with.

positions.title: Withholding Health-Critical Information Is Always Wrong
positions.text: A medical doctor in any culture who withholds health-critical information from patients is failing in professional duties.

positions.title: Mill Affords Broader Liberty Than Hobbes
positions.text: Mill's political philosophy affords citizens a maximally expansive set of liberties based on the harm principle, while Hobbes restricts liberty severely to prevent anarchy.

positions.title: Hobbes' Pessimistic View of Human Nature Justified by Historical Context
positions.text: Hobbes' dim view of human nature and need for strong authority was influenced by witnessing the English Civil War and its anarchic violence.

positions.title: Economic Freedom Requires Political Freedom
positions.text: Mill understood that political freedom (speech, assembly, religion) is necessary for economic prosperity, not just freedom to transact.

positions.title: Minimal Government Compatible with Social Order
positions.text: Contra Hobbes, minimal government intervention (beyond preventing harm) does not lead to anarchy but rather to prosperity and flourishing.

positions.title: Adam Smith's Insights Support Mill's Liberty Principle
positions.text: Adam Smith's demonstration that free economic exchange creates prosperity supports Mill's broader argument for maximal liberty.

positions.title: Excessive Legal Restriction Harms Economy and Society
positions.text: Laws that do more than prevent harm tend to create a sluggish economy, bloated government, and depressed populace.

positions.title: Hobbesian Stability Concerns Overstated
positions.text: Hobbes' fear that freedoms of speech, assembly, and religion lead to civil war and anarchy are overstated; such freedoms are compatible with stability.

positions.title: Clear and Present Danger Standard for Liberty Restrictions
positions.text: The law should only restrict liberty to prevent "clear and present dangers," not mere anticipations or possibilities (Mill's view).

positions.title: Truth Is Correspondence
positions.text: Truth is a property of representations (beliefs, statements) that consists in those representations corresponding to or accurately depicting reality.

positions.title: Truth Requires Representations
positions.text: Truth is not a property of the world itself but a relation between representations and the world. Without representations, there would be no truth (though there would be states of affairs).

positions.title: Truth Is Objective in Important Sense
positions.text: While truth requires representations (subject-dependent), once representations exist, whether they are true is determined by the world, not by us (objective in relevant sense).

positions.title: Inference to Best Explanation as Epistemic Method
positions.text: We determine whether beliefs are true by assessing whether they minimize anomalies and coincidences relative to alternatives. Beliefs that best explain available data are most likely true.

positions.title: We Cannot Compare Beliefs Directly with Reality
positions.text: We cannot directly compare beliefs with the realities they represent; we only know reality through our beliefs, so justification involves coherence and explanatory power.

positions.title: Anti-Skeptical Argument from Coherence
positions.text: We can know we're not dreaming/hallucinating because the skeptical hypothesis (mind creating all experiences ex nihilo) posits more miracles/unexplained facts than realism does.

positions.title: Mental Coherence Indicates External Reality
positions.text: The fact that our mental states are internally coherent and practically useful indicates they represent external realities whose mutual relations the relations of our experiences shadow.

positions.title: Global Skepticism Posits Too Many Miracles
positions.text: If all experiences arise ex nihilo in the mind without external support, this would be a vast collection of unexplained miracles. Hypotheses that minimize miracles are to be preferred.

positions.title: Modern Epistemology Errs in Starting with Doubt
positions.text: Modern epistemology (post-Descartes) mistakenly starts with radical doubt (Evil Demon) rather than with belief (Angel of Truth), making it artificially difficult to account for knowledge.

positions.title: Better to Explain Error in Terms of Knowledge
positions.text: Epistemology should start with paradigm cases of knowledge and explain error as deviation, rather than starting with skeptical doubt and trying to explain knowledge.

positions.title: Schopenhauer's Fractal Metaphysics A Priori More Reasonable
positions.text: Schopenhauer's view that reality has a fractal structure (micro replicates macro, with will and idea throughout) is more reasonable than materialism which makes mind an inexplicable anomaly.

positions.title: Materialism Either Absurd or Implausible
positions.text: Materialism either absurdly denies mind exists or implausibly claims mind is identical with physical processes to which it bears no apparent resemblance.

positions.title: World as Will and Idea Throughout
positions.text: Schopenhauer's conception that the world is fundamentally will and idea at all levels (not just in humans) avoids the problem of mind as inexplicable cleavage in a purely physical world.

positions.title: Religious Belief Requires Evidence
positions.text: W.K. Clifford is right that religious belief, like other beliefs, requires adequate evidential support. William James' "will to believe" defense is inadequate.

positions.title: Religious Believers vs. Skeptics Disagree on Standards of Evidence
positions.text: The debate between religious believers (like O'Reilly) and skeptics (like Dawkins) often centers on what counts as adequate evidence for God's existence.

positions.title: Arguments from Design and Fine-Tuning Have Force
positions.text: The argument that the universe's apparent design and fine-tuning for life provides some evidence for God's existence is not obviously fallacious.

positions.title: Problem of Evil Seriously Challenges Theism
positions.text: The existence and extent of suffering in the world poses a serious philosophical problem for belief in an omnipotent, omniscient, omnibenevolent God.

positions.title: Jaynes' Bicameral Mind Hypothesis Is Speculative But Interesting
positions.text: Julian Jaynes' hypothesis that ancient peoples experienced thoughts as auditory hallucinations (voices of gods) is speculative but offers interesting explanatory power for religious phenomena.

positions.title: Ancient Religious Experience Was More Direct Than Modern
positions.text: For ancient peoples, religious experiences may have been more direct and experiential (through hallucinations) rather than requiring faith in implausible doctrines.

positions.title: Modern Religiosity Lacks Psychological Force of Ancient Religion
positions.text: Modern religious belief, not being experientially grounded like ancient "bicameral" religion, lacks the psychological reality and force it had for ancients.

positions.title: Blindsight Analogy Illuminates Pre-Reflective Consciousness
positions.text: The phenomenon of blindsight (seeing without knowing one sees) helps explain how ancient peoples could be conscious without being self-conscious or reflective.

positions.title: Religious Figures Now Have "Second-Class Existence"
positions.text: For modern people, God exists as a background figure called upon in need, lacking the primary reality He had for ancients who directly experienced divine presence.

positions.title: Modern Science Risks Faustian Separation from Nature
positions.text: Modern science's attempt to rise above and dominate nature (symbolized by Faust) risks losing connection with our natural grounding.

positions.title: Neuroscience Can Potentially Increase Happiness
